root_cause: Connection-pool exhaustion caused by slow queries, not a database overload. pool-svc runs with a pool of only 5 DB connections (POOL_SIZE=5) and, because SLOW_QUERY_DISABLED was unset, every /order request executed a slow 'SELECT pg_sleep(5)' query that held its pool connection for 5 seconds. The load generator drives 12 concurrent /order workers (> POOL_SIZE), so all 5 connections stayed permanently checked out. /healthz also acquires a pool connection, so it (and new orders) could never get one and timed out with 'could not acquire connection from pool: context canceled', failing health checks. Evidence: GET /debug showed acquired=5 idle=0 total=5 with a climbing acquire_waits counter, and pg_stat_activity showed exactly 5 'active SELECT pg_sleep($1)' queries while Postgres was otherwise near-idle (pg_sleep burns no CPU) and healthy. The DB looked overloaded but was fine — the app's pool was starved by slow queries.
actions_taken: 1) Triaged: confirmed both pool-svc endpoints timed out (healthz 000/3s, order 000/8s) while all containers were 'Up' and postgres 'healthy'. 2) Read pool-svc logs ('slow query ENABLED (slow queries hold connections), pool_max=5' and repeated 'healthz: could not acquire connection from pool'). 3) Gathered evidence via /debug (5/5 connections acquired, 0 idle, rising acquire_waits) and psql pg_stat_activity (5 active pg_sleep queries, DB idle/healthy) confirming pool exhaustion rather than DB overload. 4) Read app/main.go, which documents the intended fix: SLOW_QUERY_DISABLED=1 makes /order run a fast 'SELECT 1' so connections cycle quickly; enlarging POOL_SIZE only masks the problem. 5) Applied the least-risky fix: added SLOW_QUERY_DISABLED=1 (with an explanatory comment) to the pool-svc service in tier0/docker-compose.yaml and recreated ONLY that service with 'docker compose up -d pool-svc'. 6) Verified recovery under the still-running load: /healthz returns 200 in ~3ms across repeated probes, /order returns fast {"query":"fast"}, /debug shows acquired=1 idle=4 (connections cycling), and no real pg_sleep queries remain. Confirmed the payments neighbor, postgres, load, and operator containers were left untouched.
postmortem: Blameless postmortem: pool-svc became unavailable because a small fixed connection pool (5) was fully consumed by intentionally slow business queries (pg_sleep 5s) while offered load (12 concurrent) exceeded the pool size. Since the liveness/health probe also draws from the same pool, connection starvation manifested as failed health checks and user-facing timeouts even though the database and all processes were running normally. The failure was misleading: the DB appeared to be the culprit but was near-idle — the true bottleneck was app-side pool capacity vs. per-request connection hold time. Remediation was to make the request path release connections quickly (fast query), which restored throughput immediately without scaling the DB or the pool. What went well: the service exposed a /debug pool-stat endpoint and clear logs, making diagnosis fast; the fix was config-only and scoped to a single service. What went wrong / risks: a single shared pool for health checks and business traffic means one slow code path can take down liveness signaling; the pool was also undersized relative to configured concurrency with no acquire backpressure or timeout tuning. Follow-ups: (1) isolate health checks from the business pool (dedicated connection or non-pool check) so liveness never depends on business-query latency; (2) treat slow queries as the real defect — add query timeouts, fix/avoid genuinely slow queries (e.g. missing indexes) rather than only enlarging pools; (3) set pool size and acquire timeouts deliberately relative to expected concurrency and add load-shedding/backpressure; (4) add alerting on pool saturation metrics (acquired vs max, acquire_waits) to catch exhaustion before health checks fail.
